**[19:55](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=1195)** — and an alarm will sound once the speaker's time has expired. And with that, our first speaker of the night is Angie Rogers.

**[20:41](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=1241)** — - Can I (indistinct)? - Yeah. Thank you. - Good evening, my name is Angie Rogers, and I live in Coralville. I'm here tonight to speak about the proposed '26-'27 budget reductions and specifically the priorities reflected in the proposal presented by administration. I understand the district must reduce spending by at least $6 million. I do not minimize the difficulty of this reality, and I understand that no set of reductions will be painless. But what concerns me is not simply that cuts are being made. What concerns me is where they are being made first and how they are being justified. When we review the proposed reductions, the most concrete and clearly identified cuts are overwhelmingly student-facing. Elementary classroom teachers, $900,000. Middle school staff, $500,000.

**[21:27](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=1287)** — High school staff, $600,000. Non-renewal of selected probationary teachers, $1 million. Pre-K through 12 support staff, $600,000. Special education, lead support positions, $360,000. Building substitutes, $200,000. Reduction or elimination of attendance support busing, 700,000 to $1 million. That is approximately $5 million in identified cuts directly affecting classrooms, instruction, and student support. By contrast, administrative reductions are described as approximately $2 million, but those reductions appear far less specific and largely contingent on reassignment or attrition. That is not the same level of sacrifice. Students and teachers will feel these cuts immediately. Central administration will not.

**[22:12](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=1332)** — If this district is facing true financial problems, then we must be willing to consider structural changes at every level of the district, including central office administration. The core mission of a school district is to educate students. That means the areas cut last should be the ones that directly impact the mission. Non-student-facing administrative costs should be prioritized for reduction first. There are many options. There could be pay cuts for the executive cabinet. There could also be restructuring and consolidation of leadership roles. In the short term, we could also outsource and have fractional positions for HR, CFO, and even the superintendent. These changes would have no immediate impact on classrooms. Before we cut teachers and increase class sizes, why are we not publicly presenting a full central office organizational chart

**[23:00](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=1380)** — and compensation breakdown. Before reducing special education support, why aren't all consulting contracts frozen except for legal and tax? Before asking teachers to ration copy paper and toner, why are top administrative perks, including car allowances, cell phone allowances, internet allowances, and other contractual benefits, not being reviewed or even voluntarily suspended? These are not symbolic questions. They are governance questions. They are priority questions. And they go directly to public trust. Families are watching. Staff are watching. Taxpayers are watching. Budgets reflect values. Budgets reveal what an institution chooses to protect and what it chooses not to. If classrooms absorb the majority of reductions while central administration remains insulated,

**[23:46](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=1426)** — that sends a clear message and is not one that builds confidence in district leadership or this board. I am asking each board member to take a hard look at the proposed cuts and reconsider the priorities. Protect classrooms first. Exhaust central administration restructuring options. Consider temporary pay cuts and benefit freezes at the top before implementing permanent classroom reductions. Apply the same standard of transparency to central administration that is applied to teachers and building staff. Our students should not be the district's primary budget balancing tool. Thank you. - [President Malone] Thank you. (audience applauding) Next up, we will have Jim Walters.

**[33:01](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=1981)** — - Hi, my name is Ashton Barrett, and I'm the parent of a second grader and a kindergartner at Twain. In light of the recent budget issues, I am here this evening to ask the district to assess the effectiveness of their student technology platforms and to consider cutting the one-to-one Chromebook program for elementary students. The board approved 6 million last year for the purchase of new Chromebooks, and the district has previously stated they spend around 1.5 million annually on Chromebooks. I don't know what Happy Numbers and Lexia cost, but I'd guess it's in the six figures. That is a significant investment and makes me wonder what is the academic ROI for these programs. And are there more cost effective alternatives that are just as successful, if not more so,

**[33:47](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2027)** — at teaching our kids this content? It is challenging to get my children to tell me what they're learning from these programs. But my second grader has shared that her desk mate enters six-seven for every answer on Happy Numbers until the program finally spits out the correct answer for her. When my kids do talk about these programs, it's mostly about what levels they and their classmates are on. The underlying judgmental nature of their comments concern me as they tend to be either self-critical because they're on a lower level, or gloating because they're ahead of their peers. I worry about the impact of these gamified tech programs on my kids' intrinsic motivation to learn, their sense of self, and on social dynamics in the classroom.

**[34:32](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2072)** — One of the reasons I choose to send my kids to school here is the district's mission to ensure all students will become responsible, independent learners, capable of making informed decisions in a democratic society as well as in the dynamic global community. Much research suggests, and I personally believe, that embodied experiential and interpersonal learning is the most effective in creating responsible, independent learners. I am grateful because I see this type of learning in my children's classrooms, and I think we could maximize it by ditching one-to-one Chromebooks. Instead of spending millions to have classrooms of kids with headphones on, glued to their Chromebooks, doing Happy Numbers, what if we had groups of students playing dominoes, Go Fish, Cribbage, and Yahtzee.

**[35:18](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2118)** — Students would not only learn math skills, but they would also learn critical social skills like taking turns, teamwork, and how to regulate their bodies and emotions to play quietly while their teachers work with other students. What if instead of computer programs teaching our kids literacy, we had reading buddy programs, where older students were paired with younger students to read aloud and do literacy activities together? Not only would they learn, but older students would build skill mastery and confidence by stepping into the role of teacher. Younger kids would feel special by engaging with the cooler older kids. And the sense of school community would be enriched by these cross-grade relationships. What if instead of one-to-one Chromebooks, we returned to computer labs,

**[36:04](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2164)** — where students learned essential tech skills for the 21st century, and they also received education in digital safety and citizenship, and how to maintain their health and wellbeing in a screen-filled world? Author Wendell Berry said, "I am not against technology so much as I am for community." I would add, I am not against technology so much as I am for student learning and school community. I think ending the one-to-one elementary Chromebook policy would be a win-win-win. It would be a win for our budget, a win for our students' learning and wellbeing, and a win for enriching our school culture and community. Thank you. - [President Malone] Thank you. (audience applauding) Next up, we have Jenna Schlegel-Preheim.

**[36:52](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2212)** — - [Attendee] Thank you.

**[36:57](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2217)** — - Hi, I'm Jenna Schlegel-Preheim. It's good to see you all. I have two kids at Twain Elementary School, and I'm a mental health professional. I have deep respect for each of you and the work that you do taking care of our district. Thank you. We have the most fabulous experience with our neighborhood school, Twain Elementary. We have experienced a deep and satisfying community there. Our kids's teachers are extremely experienced and wise, and it's such a great feeling to send our kids to school each day to teachers that we deeply trust. We love our principal who brings her fabulous energy and total genuine commitment to our students. Twain is vital to our neighborhood. I know budget concerns are a multifaceted issue

**[37:43](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2263)** — with need for many solutions. As one of those solutions, I hope that you'll consider retiring the one-to-one Chromebooks and EdTech subscriptions at the elementary school level. Jared Cooney Horvath is a neuroscientist and specializes in human learning. This is his book, "The Digital Delusion: How Classroom Technology Harms Our Kids' Learning and How to Help Them Thrive Again." It's a great book, highly recommend. He cites research across the world that shows that learning outcomes actually decrease with more devices and EdTech. Across three major international assessments, higher computer use in school correlates with lower learning scores. Students who use computers at school consistently,

**[38:28](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2308)** — who do not use computers at school consistently score higher. His research also shows that as money spent on devices and EdTech increases, scores on the national assessment of educational progress decrease. As money for EdTech goes up, learning goes down. The author cites three main reasons for this. One, we are teaching kids to fragment their attention, which is at odds with learning. Two, empathy between teachers and students, which is a relational, biological phenomenon and an extremely potent ingredient of learning, is interrupted with screens. And three, things learned on a screen are more difficult to transfer to other contexts.

**[39:14](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2354)** — On a personal level, the gamification of learning is concerning to me because I don't want my kids's beautiful intrinsic motivation to wither or for them to eventually find that they are bored during regular class time because they aren't receiving the dopamine hit of a fancy banner letting them know that they've leveled up. In short, our kids would flourish from less screen time at school. And discontinuing one-to-one Chromebooks and EdTech subscriptions would save money. I would love to help work toward this goal in whatever way possible. I'm hoping that you'll ask me to be on a working group someday, and thank you so much. - [President Malone] Thank you. **[47:19](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2839)** — - Royceann Porter, 136 Appanoose Court, Iowa City.

**[47:30](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2850)** — Good evening, board members and community. My name is Royceann Porter. I am here tonight because I have worked alongside superintendent Matt Degner since day one of his leadership in this district. I have seen firsthand what it looks like when a leader doesn't just use the word equity as a buzz word but lives it as a mission. For many Black families in this community, trust hasn't always existed with institutions. It had to be built through listening, consistency, and real action, and that is exactly what Matt has done. For years, we asked for our children to be seen, heard, and supported. Under Matt's leadership, the Black Voices Project wasn't treated as a side effort. It became a priority. He didn't just open the door. He pulled up a chair and made sure

**[48:16](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2896)** — the community had a real seat at the table. Because of this commitment to DEI work, families began engaging instead of withdrawing. Students felt valued instead of invisible. And the culture of this district started shifting in meaningful ways toward accountability and belonging. Recently, I watched something that was deeply troubling. When concerns were raised and names were listed, Board Chair Ruthina Malone was placed in the line of fire, not because she did anything wrong but simply because her name appeared. And let's be honest, when equity work gets challenged, too often Black leadership becomes the target. Instead of focusing on the real work being done to support students, blame gets redirected, excuse me,

**[49:03](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2943)** — in ways that discourage progress and silence voices that are finally being heard. That is not leadership, and that is not how we move forward as a community. Matt didn't retreat when things got uncomfortable. He didn't shift blame. He stayed engaged, accountable, and committed. Leadership is more than budgets and policies. It's about protecting progress, supporting those doing the work, and standing firm when the pressure rises. Matt listens, Matt collaborates, and Matt acts. And because of that, families trust this district more today than they did years ago. I know hard budget conversations are happening. I respect that responsibility. But you cannot put a price on trust. You cannot measure belonging on a spreadsheet.

**[49:48](https://youtu.be/7Vf3gNZbtM4?t=2988)** — And you cannot afford to cut the very programs that are finally making our schools safer and more inclusive for Black and brown students. He has centered equity with action, not just words. I urge this board to protect the progress we've made. Do not lose the momentum, and do not lose the leader who helped create it. Thank you. - [President Malone] Thank you. Next up, we'll have David Noerper.
